content:
A side project that was supposed to need Redux ended up shipping with zero state libraries. Here is what replaced them.

4 min read
1 hour ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

I spent an afternoon installing Redux Toolkit on a side project last summer.
The app had four routes. A login screen, a list, a detail page, a settings panel. The kind of project you can sketch on a napkin. I added Redux because that is what you do for a React app. Somewhere around the third reducer file, I stopped to ask myself what problem I was actually solving.
I deleted Redux that evening. The app shipped a week later. Six months on, I have not added a state management library back. I have not needed one.
This article is what replaced it. Six native React patterns that quietly cover most of what state libraries promise to solve. None of them are clever. All of them are old. The trick is matching the right one to the right kind of state.
The diagnosis came from counting
When I audited the four React apps I had built with state libraries over the previous two years, the pattern was consistent.
